{{Short description|Men's association football team}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=September 2024}}

| First game     = {{fb|Malta|1943}} 2–3 {{fb-rt|AUT}} <br/> ([[Gżira]], [[Malta]]; 24 February 1957)
| Largest win    = {{fb|MLT}} 7–1 {{fb-rt|LIE}} <br/> ([[Ta' Qali]], [[Malta]]; 26 March 2008)
| Largest loss   = {{fb|ESP}} [[Spain 12–1 Malta|12–1]] {{fb-rt|MLT}} <br/> ([[Seville]], [[Spain]]; 21 December 1983)
}}

The '''Malta national football team''' ({{langx|mt|Tim nazzjonali tal-futbol ta' Malta}}) represents [[Malta]] in men's international [[Association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Malta Football Association]], the governing body for [[football in Malta]].

The first official game played by Malta was a 3–2 defeat in a [[exhibition game|friendly]] against [[Austria national football team|Austria]] in 1957.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20070206/sport/maltas-late-rally-thrills-home-crowd-on-debut.27363 |title=Malta's late rally thrills home crowd on debut |newspaper=Times of Malta |first=Carmel |last=Baldacchino |date=6 February 2007 |access-date=7 October 2018 |archive-date=7 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181007223431/https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20070206/sport/maltas-late-rally-thrills-home-crowd-on-debut.27363 |url-status=live }}</ref> Their competitive debut arrived five years later, playing against [[Denmark national football team|Denmark]] in the [[1964 European Nations' Cup qualifying|preliminary round]] of the [[1964 European Nations' Cup]].<ref name="all-time-results">{{cite web |url=https://www.mfa.com.mt/en/national-team-all-results.htm |title=National Team All Time Results |publisher=Malta Football Association |access-date=2 September 2011 |archive-date=6 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200506004818/https://www.mfa.com.mt/en/national-team-all-results.htm |url-status=dead }}</ref> Malta have competed in every qualifier for the [[UEFA European Championship|European Championship]] since 1964 (except 1968) and [[FIFA World Cup]] since 1974, but have never made it to any finals of all major international competition.

== History ==

Malta played its first international game on 24 February 1957 at the [[Empire Stadium (Gżira)|Empire Stadium]], losing 3-2 to [[Austria national football team|Austria]]. That match was played in front of a capacity crowd at the old Empire Stadium. The [[Malta Football Association]] joined [[FIFA]] in 1959 and [[UEFA]] a year later. In late 1959, Malta played in the [[Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics – Men's African Qualifiers|1960 Summer Olympics African Qualifiers]] against [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]] and [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]], in which they finished last in the group with two draws and two losses. Since then, Malta has largely remained as one of Europe's weakest teams, though its fortunes have increased since late 2010s.

The Maltese international side first competed in the qualifying rounds of the [[UEFA European Championship|UEFA European Nations Cup]] in 1962, and in [[FIFA World Cup qualification]] in 1971.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.mfa.com.mt/maltafootballassociation/content.aspx?id=96399 |title=History of the Malta Football Association |publisher=[[Malta Football Association]] |access-date=28 July 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110927140807/http://www.mfa.com.mt/maltafootballassociation/content.aspx?id=96399 |archive-date=27 September 2011 }}</ref> Malta's first competitive draw ended 1–1 against Greece in 1970. Malta's first two competitive wins were victories of 2–0 and 2–1 at home to [[Greece national football team|Greece]] and [[Iceland national football team|Iceland]] in European Championship qualifiers in 1975 and 1982 respectively. In 1979, Malta drew 0-0 with West Germany in a European championship qualifier and they met again on 16 December 1984 for a memorable World Cup Qualifier in front of a record attendance at the Ta'Qali stadium, where the 1982 & eventual 1986 World Cup runners-up only managed a 2–3 win. Another prestigious result was achieved in March 1987 when Malta drew  2-2 in Portugal, in a qualifier for Euro'88 and the side also twice drew against [[Hungary national football team|Hungary]] during the [[1990 FIFA World Cup qualification|qualification for the 1990 FIFA World Cup]], and recorded four friendly wins during 1991 and 1992.<ref name="all-time-results" />

Malta's third competitive win came with a 1–0 victory away to [[Estonia national football team|Estonia]] in a 1993 [[Football World Cup|World Cup]] qualifier in which Kris Laferla scored.<ref name=Independent>{{cite news |url=http://www.independent.com.mt/news2.asp?artid=40420 |last=Brincat |first=Henry |title=Malta 2–1 Hungary |work=[[The Malta Independent]] |date=15 October 2006 |access-date=29 July 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140109095249/http://www.independent.com.mt/news2.asp?artid=40420 |archive-date=9 January 2014 |url-status=dead }}</ref> In October 1994 Malta held Czech Republic 0–0 in a qualifier for the [[UEFA Euro 1996]], in which the latter ended runners-up. Six years later, in October 2000, in a group qualifying match for the 2002 World Cup, once again Malta managed another 0–0 draw vs Czech Republic which eventually cost the latter a place at the following major tournament. In June 2000, Malta played [[England national football team|England]], then managed by [[Kevin Keegan]]. Trailing 2–1 going into the final minutes, Malta were awarded a penalty, however [[David Carabott]]'s effort was saved by [[Richard Wright (footballer)|Richard Wright]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/euro2000/teams/england/775879.stm|title=Malta v England: Clockwatch|date=3 June 2000|access-date=14 May 2014|work=BBC|archive-date=30 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140330085145/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/euro2000/teams/england/775879.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> Through November 2001 and May 2002, Malta played and remained undefeated in 6 international matches and in between they won the locally hosted (Rothmans) International Tournament. During 2005, Malta drew 1–1 against [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]] and [[Bulgaria national football team|Bulgaria]]. Another positive result was the 1–1 home draw in a friendly match against [[Northern Ireland national football team|Northern Ireland]], though [[George Mallia]] missed an injury time penalty which would have given them a win.<ref>{{cite web
|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/internationals/4159940.stm
|title=Malta 1–1 Northern Ireland
|publisher=[[BBC]]
|date=17 August 2005
|access-date=15 September 2015
|archive-date=29 May 2006
|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060529105733/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/internationals/4159940.stm
|url-status=live
}}</ref> On 11 October 2006, Malta managed another competitive victory, a 2–1 triumph over Hungary in the [[UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying#Group C|European Championship qualifying]] with [[André Schembri]] scoring twice.<ref name=Independent />

On 7 February 2007, Malta drew 1–1 with one of the hosts of Euro 2008, Austria. The game was played to commemorate the 50th anniversary of the first international match played by the Maltese national team.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.independent.com.mt/news2.asp?artid=62384 |title=Malta to play Euro 2008 co-hosts Austria on 30 May |work=[[The Malta Independent]] |date=22 December 2007 |access-date=2 September 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120330020617/http://www.independent.com.mt/news2.asp?artid=62384 |archive-date=30 March 2012 |url-status=dead }}</ref> On 8 September 2007, Malta managed another draw against [[Turkey national football team|Turkey]] in a Euro 2008 qualifying match, the game finishing 2–2.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://uk.reuters.com/article/uk-soccer-euro-idUKL083567320070909 |last=Huggins |first=Trevor |title=England notch easy win against Israel |work=[[Reuters UK]] |date=9 September 2007}}{{dead link|date=September 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> On 26 March 2008, Malta achieved its largest ever victory, a 7–1 defeat of [[Liechtenstein national football team|Liechtenstein]] in a friendly at the [[National Stadium, Ta' Qali|Ta' Qali Stadium]], with [[Michael Mifsud]] scoring five goals.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20080327/sport/five-goal-mifsud-inspires-malta-to-massive-win.201718 |title=Five-goal Mifsud inspires Malta to massive win |work=The [[Times of Malta]] |date=27 March 2008 |access-date=29 July 2011 |archive-date=4 March 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120304173922/http://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20080327/sport/five-goal-mifsud-inspires-malta-to-massive-win.201718 |url-status=live }}</ref> A 2–0 friendly win over [[Georgia national football team|Georgia]] followed in 2009.<ref name=FIFA />

In May 2010, sponsorship of the Maltese national side was taken on by sportswear firm Givova, who also designed a range of new kits for the team.<ref name=":0">{{cite news |url=http://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20100511/football/high-profile-friendly-a-useful-test-buttigieg.306743 |title=High-profile friendly a useful test&nbsp;– Buttigieg |work=The Times of Malta |date=11 May 2010 |access-date=29 July 2011 |archive-date=9 October 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111009081843/http://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20100511/football/high-profile-friendly-a-useful-test-buttigieg.306743 |url-status=live }}</ref> One month later, however, the side had fallen to their lowest ever FIFA world ranking position, of 169th in the world.<ref name=FIFA>{{cite web |url=https://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/ranking/news/newsid=1413516.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110410233959/http://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/ranking/news/newsid=1413516.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=10 April 2011 |title=Malta make ground from Swiss denial |publisher=fifa.com |date=7 April 2011}}</ref> In 2009, Malta had a 0–0 draw with Albania at home. This was their only point for the 2010 World Cup qualifying. On 11 August 2010, Malta drew 1–1 at home against [[North Macedonia national football team|Macedonia]] in a friendly game, with Michael Mifsud scoring a brilliant diving header for Malta.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20100811/sport/malta-macedonia-draw-1-1.321999 |title=Malta-Macedonia draw 1–1 |work=The Times of Malta |date=11 August 2010 |access-date=29 July 2011 |archive-date=3 September 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180903013332/https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20100811/sport/malta-macedonia-draw-1-1.321999 |url-status=live }}</ref>

In February 2011, the national football team of Malta achieved a 0–0 draw against [[Switzerland national football team|Switzerland]], in which goalkeeper [[Justin Haber]] saved two penalties.<ref name="FIFA" /> On 6 September 2011, Malta won their first [[UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying|Euro 2012 qualifying]] point, with a 1–1 draw against Georgia. During the years of 2010 and 2011, Malta did not get many positive results, and coach John Buttigieg and assistant coach Carmel Busittil were both sacked in October 2011.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.espn.com/sports/soccer/news/_/id/7150827/malta-fires-john-buttigieg-national-coach |title=Malta fires national coach John Buttigieg |publisher=[[ESPN]] |date=26 October 2011 |access-date=2 March 2012 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304234418/http://espn.go.com/sports/soccer/news/_/id/7150827/malta-fires-john-buttigieg-national-coach |url-status=live }}</ref> For the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification]] campaign, Malta won their first World Cup qualifying match in 20 years, nabbing a 1–0 win over [[Armenia national football team|Armenia]] in June 2013.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20130607/football/malta-armenia.472938 |title=Resilient Malta stun Armenia |newspaper=Times of Malta |date=7 June 2013 |first=Kevin |last=Azzopardi |access-date=2 September 2018 |archive-date=3 September 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180903013304/https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20130607/football/malta-armenia.472938 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=":0" /> In June 2017, Malta defeated [[Ukraine national football team|Ukraine]] 1–0 in a friendly match, thanks to a lone goal by defender [[Zach Muscat]].<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.independent.com.mt/articles/2017-06-06/football/Friendly-match-Malta-obtains-encouraging-win-in-Ukraine-6736175172 |title=Friendly match: Malta obtains encouraging win against Ukraine |newspaper=The Malta Independent |date=6 June 2017 |access-date=2 September 2018 |archive-date=2 September 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180902220823/http://www.independent.com.mt/articles/2017-06-06/football/Friendly-match-Malta-obtains-encouraging-win-in-Ukraine-6736175172 |url-status=live }}</ref> Malta recorded their 7th all-time competitive win (and their first in the UEFA Nations League) on 13 October 2020, defeating Latvia 1–0 in Riga, scoring the winning goal in the 97th minute.

Malta went on to deliver several good performances in the times that followed. On 14 November 2020 in the UEFA Nations League, Malta earned a 3–1 home victory against Faroe Islands, thanks in part to a great goal from [[Jurgen Degabriele]], showcasing early signs of momentum in the 2020–21 campaign. In 2021, Malta recorded a standout result in FIFA World Cup qualifying with a comfortable 3–0 home win over Cyprus on 1 September 2021, featuring two goals from [[Cain Attard]], a rare competitive triumph that boosted morale in a challenging Group H campaign. The 2022 calendar year proved to be Malta's most successful in recent times: across ten matches they achieved 5 wins, 1 draw and 4 losses, scoring 12 goals and conceding 9, marking an uptick in form. On 5 June 2022, they opened the 2022–23 UEFA Nations League with a 2–0 away win over San Marino, with [[Matthew Guillaumier]] scoring Malta’s opener in Serravalle and setting a confident tone for the campaign. In September 2022, Malta delivered perhaps their most celebrated recent result: a 2–1 friendly victory over Israel at Ta’ Qali National Stadium, with goals by [[Alexander Satariano]] (84') and [[Ferdinando Apap]] (87'), overturning Israel’s early penalty to secure the win against a traditionally stronger side.

In more recent years, Malta did achieve a handful of positive results. In June 2023 (UEFA Nations League 2022–23, Group D2), Malta earned a 2–0 away win over San Marino on 5 June, followed by a 1–0 home victory against San Marino on 12 June, anchoring a strong run in their Group D2 campaign and finishing second overall in the group. In September 2024, during the 2024–25 UEFA Nations League, Malta beat Moldova 2–0 away on 7 September and then overcame Andorra 1–0 away on 10 September, with the goal against Andorra scored by [[Ryan Camenzuli]] in the 44th minute. Although positive results have been harder to come by for Malta during 2024 and 2025, they did get another competitive win, securing a 1–0 home win against Moldova on 13 October 2024, courtesy of an 87th‑minute penalty from Teuma, again in UEFA Nations League Group D2. Moving into 2025 World Cup qualifying, Malta registered a 0–0 draw at home against Lithuania on 7 June 2025, showing resilience at Ta’ Qali despite admittedly tougher opposition. On 14th November 2025, in their penultimate match of FIFA World Cup Qualification, Malta caused one of the greatest ever World Cup Qualifying upsets in a 1-0 away victory over highly-favoured [[Finland national football team|Finland]] in [[Helsinki]]. After the match, Maltese goalkeeper [[Henry Bonello]] was given praise for his "unbeatable" performance, which included nine saves.

== Team Image ==

=== Stadium ===

Malta's spiritual home is the [[National Stadium, Ta' Qali|National Stadium]], the largest stadium in Malta.<ref name="National Stadium">{{cite web|url=https://www.mfa.com.mt/about-us/facilities/national-stadium/ |title=National Stadium |publisher=[[Malta Football Association]] |access-date=19 March 2025}}</ref> The highest ever attendance for Malta's matches at Ta Qali was in a [[1986 FIFA World Cup qualification|1986 World Cup qualifier]] against [[Germany national football team|West Germany]], where 35,102 spectators turned up for the match.<ref name="National Stadium"/>

Before Ta' Qali, Malta used to play its home matches at the [[Empire Stadium (Gżira)|Empire Stadium]], which was infamous for its hard surface.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://timesofmalta.com/article/watch-a-walk-through-gziras-stadium-of-dreams.698448 |title=Watch: A walk through Gżira's stadium of dreams |newspaper=Times of Malta |first=Carmel |last=Baldacchino |date=5 January 2019 |access-date=19 March 2026 }}</ref> The record attendance at the Empire Stadium was 29,751, in a [[UEFA Euro 1972 qualifying|1972 UEFA Euro qualifier]] against [[England national football team|England]].

Malta also played friendly matches at the [[Hibernians Stadium]], [[Matthew Micallef St. John Athletics Stadium|Marsa Stadium]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://eu-football.info/_match.php?id=8108|title=Malta national team v Tunisia, 1 February 1983|website=Eu-football.info|accessdate=16 October 2017}}</ref> and [[Manoel Island Football Ground]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.timesofmalta.com/articles/view/20160804/football/malta-beat-libya-home-and-away.620966|title=Malta beat Libya home and away|date=4 August 2016|website=TimesofMalta.com|accessdate=21 October 2017}}</ref>.

|}

=== Supporters ===

The [[South End Core]] is a non-profit football supporters' association for the Maltese national football team and other disciplines, including [[Malta national rugby union team|rugby union]] and [[Malta men's national water polo team|water polo]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://timesofmalta.com/article/south-end-core-a-driving-force-behind-national-team.2580 |title=Watch: South End Core - a driving force behind national team |newspaper=Times of Malta |first=Carmel |last=Baldacchino |date=9 October 2007 |access-date=19 March 2026 }}</ref> The South End Core was founded in 2007 and derives its name from the fact that originally they used to occupy the South Stand of the National Stadium.

=== 2026 ===
{{football box collapsible|format=1
|date              = 26 March

== Coaching staff ==
{{updated|13 December 2024.|<ref>{{cite news |url=https://mfa.com.mt/en/news/news/4416/capece-and-zanasi-join-national-teams-coaching-staff.htm |title=Capece and Zanasi join national team's coaching staff |publisher=Malta Football Association |date=9 July 2020 |access-date=9 July 2020 |archive-date=12 July 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200712190713/https://mfa.com.mt/en/news/news/4416/capece-and-zanasi-join-national-teams-coaching-staff.htm |url-status=live }}</ref>}}

{| class="wikitable"
|-
| Head coach 
| {{flagicon|ITA}} [[Emilio De Leo]]
|-
|Assistant coach
| {{flagicon|ITA}} Mirko Valdifiori
|-
| Goalkeeping coach
| {{flagicon|ITA}} Mario Capece
|-
| Technical coordinator
| {{flagicon|MLT}} [[Ivan Woods]]
|-
|rowspan="2"|Fitness coaches
| {{flagicon|ITA}} Luca Pagani
|-
| {{flagicon|ITA}} Francesco Zanasi
|-
| Match analyst
| {{flagicon|ARG}} Facundo Styk
|-
| Team manager
| {{flagicon|MLT}} Keith Fenech
|-
|}

== Players ==
=== Current squad ===
The following players were called-up to the provisional squad for the friendly games against [[Slovakia national football team|Slovakia]] and [[Azerbaijan national football team|Azerbaijan]] on 1 and 5 June 2026.<ref> {{cite web |title=Slovakia-Malta |url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/friendlies/match/2048224--slovakia-vs-malta/lineups/ |website=UEFA |access-date=1 June 2026}}</ref><ref> {{cite web |title=Azerbaijan-Malta |url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/friendlies/match/2048230--azerbaijan-vs-malta/ |website=UEFA |access-date=1 June 2026}}</ref>

''Caps and goals correct as of 5 June 2026, after the friendly game against [[Azerbaijan national football team|Azerbaijan]].''

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Kyrian Nwoko]]|age={{bda|1997|7|4|df=y}}|caps=38|goals=4|club=[[Sliema Wanderers F.C.|Sliema Wanderers]]|clubnat=MLT|latest=v.{{fb|SMR}}, 9 September 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> Player is injured<br/>
<sup>COV</sup> Withdrew due to covid<br/>
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby<br/>
<sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team<br/>
<sup>SUS</sup> Serving suspension<br/>
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to non-injury issue.
{{nat fs end}}

== Player records ==
{{main|List of Malta international footballers|List of Malta national football team captains}}

:{{updated|1 June 2026.}}<ref name="rsssf malta player rcords">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/malt-recintlp.html |title=Malta&nbsp;– Record International Players |first=Roberto |last=Mamrud |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=8 June 2014 |archive-date=10 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220710163219/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/malt-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Malta.''

== Head-to-head record ==

{{updated|5 June 2026.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eloratings.net/Malta|title=World Football Elo Ratings: Malta|publisher=Elo Ratings|access-date=12 August 2017|archive-date=29 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180129004405/http://www.eloratings.net/Malta|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://eu-football.info/_matches.php?id=136&data=1 |title=Malta's Head to Head stats against all teams |website=eu-football.info |access-date=27 March 2022 |archive-date=8 April 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230408072951/https://eu-football.info/_matches.php?id=136&data=1 |url-status=live }}</ref>}}

** '''Champions (2):''' 1992, 2002
